summary:
The Han Dynasty (206 BC – 220 AD) was China's second imperial dynasty and the template for all subsequent Chinese imperial civilization — a 400-year era that consolidated and expanded the Qin administrative framework, established Confucianism as the state ideology, opened the [[event:1129|Silk Road Establishment]] Silk Road to Central Asia, and created a unified Chinese cultural and ethnic identity so enduring that the dominant ethnic group of China still calls itself 'Han people' (Hanzu). The Han dynasty divided into the Western Han (206 BC–9 AD) and Eastern Han (25–220 AD), interrupted by the brief Xin dynasty of Wang Mang.

The early Han adopted a pragmatic approach under the 'Huang-Lao' (Yellow Emperor-Laozi) Daoist ideology of minimal government and reduced taxes, allowing China to recover from the civil wars that ended the Qin. [[event:1136|Liu Bang Emperor Gaozu of Han]] founded the dynasty; the great expansion came under [[event:1127|Emperor Wu of Han]] (141–87 BC), who dispatched [[event:1128|Zhang Qian's Western Missions]] to Central Asia, waged the [[event:1130|Han Xiongnu Wars]] against the steppe nomads, established [[event:1131|Confucianism as State Ideology]] through the imperial examination for officials, created the [[event:1232|Han Salt and Iron Monopoly]], and oversaw the flourishing of arts and scholarship documented by [[event:1132|Sima Qian's Records]].

The Han created the Silk Road not merely as a trade route but as a diplomatic and imperial assertion — Chinese silk, lacquer, and bronze goods flowed west; horses, glass, and exotic products flowed east. Han bureaucratic examinations in Confucian learning created a meritocratic administrative class (in theory) that became the model for Chinese governance. The Han's eventual collapse in 220 AD — after a long period of eunuch domination, peasant revolts (the [[event:2836|Yellow Turban Rebellion]]), and warlord fragmentation — led to the [[event:2046|Three Kingdoms Period]], but the Han cultural achievement remained the gold standard of Chinese civilization.
